sourceTitle: The status property gives you clarity on task progress

questions.question: What are the three default categories that the Notion status property provides out of the box?

options.text: Planned, Ongoing, Finished
options.explanation: These are not the default categories; they are made‑up terms.

options.text: To Do, In Progress, Complete
options.explanation: Correct – Notion pre‑loads these three tags for every status property.

options.text: Backlog, Active, Archived
options.explanation: These are common workflow terms but not the default status tags.

options.text: Open, Closed, Deferred
options.explanation: These are not the three default categories in Notion’s status property.

questions.question: Why might a team prefer using the status property over a regular Select or Multi‑select property for tracking project stages?

options.text: Because it allows unlimited free‑form entries, encouraging creativity.
options.explanation: The status property actually restricts entry to predefined tags, reducing chaos.

options.text: It enforces a standardized set of main categories while still permitting custom sub‑categories, reducing inconsistency.
options.explanation: Correct – the property balances structure (fixed main categories) with flexibility (custom sub‑tags).

options.text: It automatically generates a Gantt chart for every project.
options.explanation: Status properties do not create Gantt charts; that's a separate view type.

options.text: It hides completed tasks from all views by default.
options.explanation: Visibility depends on filters, not on the property itself.

questions.question: Which of the following actions is NOT possible when customizing a status property?

options.text: Renaming the three main categories (To Do, In Progress, Complete).
options.explanation: Correct – the three top‑level categories are locked and cannot be renamed.

options.text: Adding a new sub‑category under In Progress.
options.explanation: You can add custom sub‑categories beneath any main category.

options.text: Changing the color of a status tag.
options.explanation: Colors are fully customizable for each tag.

options.text: Choosing to display the status as a checkbox instead of a select.
options.explanation: The property can be shown as a checkbox, which marks all To‑Do items as unchecked.

questions.question: When creating a board view that groups projects by status sub‑category, which additional option should you enable to keep the board tidy?

options.text: Hide empty groups
options.explanation: Enabling this hides any status groups that have no items, making the board cleaner.

options.text: Enable auto‑archive
options.explanation: Auto‑archive is not a board setting in Notion.

options.text: Show completed tasks at the top
options.explanation: Sorting order is controlled separately; this option does not exist.

options.text: Lock the board layout
options.explanation: There is no lock feature for board layouts.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of using the status property’s built‑in constraints when filtering a database?

options.text: It allows you to filter by multiple unrelated tags in a single step.
options.explanation: Multiple unrelated tags still require separate filter clauses.

options.text: It reduces the risk of missing a similarly‑named tag, ensuring filter results are accurate.
options.explanation: Because tags are standardized, you won’t accidentally omit a variant when filtering.

options.text: It automatically updates all filtered views when a tag’s color changes.
options.explanation: Color changes do not affect filter logic.

options.text: It groups all tasks into a single “All Statuses” view.
options.explanation: Grouping is a separate step; the property itself doesn’t create an “All Statuses” view.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a custom sub‑category you might add under the "To Do" main category?

options.text: Blocked
options.explanation: Blocked is listed as a suggested custom sub‑category for To Do.

options.text: Published
options.explanation: Published belongs under the Complete category.

options.text: With (team member)
options.explanation: This sub‑category is suggested for In Progress.

options.text: Ready to Publish
options.explanation: Ready to Publish is a sub‑category under Complete.

questions.question: If you display the status property as a checkbox, how does Notion treat items labeled "To Do"?

options.text: They appear checked, indicating they are complete.
options.explanation: Checkboxes show To Do items as unchecked, not checked.

options.text: They appear unchecked, mirroring a typical to‑do list.
options.explanation: Correct – the checkbox view treats all To Do entries as unchecked.

options.text: They are hidden from the view.
options.explanation: Checkbox display does not hide items.

options.text: They are automatically moved to the In Progress category.
options.explanation: Changing the display type does not change the underlying status.

questions.question: What problem does the article highlight about using a Multi‑select property for status tracking?

options.text: It forces every task to have exactly three tags.
options.explanation: Multi‑select allows any number of tags; the issue is different.

options.text: Users can create overlapping or duplicate tags, leading to inconsistent filtering.
options.explanation: Correct – the article notes that free‑form entry creates confusion and duplicate categories.

options.text: It disables the ability to group tasks by status.
options.explanation: Grouping is still possible, though less reliable due to inconsistent tags.

options.text: It automatically deletes any custom tags after 30 days.
options.explanation: There is no auto‑deletion behavior described.

questions.question: Which step is NOT part of creating a filtered view that shows only projects that are "In Progress"?

options.text: Select Add view and choose a name.
options.explanation: This is a required first step.

options.text: Click Filter and choose the status property.
options.explanation: Filtering by status is essential for the view.

options.text: Set the filter condition to Status is In Progress.
options.explanation: This condition defines the view.

options.text: Enable the board grouping option before adding the filter.
options.explanation: Board grouping is optional and not required for a simple filtered list view.

questions.question: How does the status property improve a manager’s ability to see the "bigger picture" of project work?

options.text: It automatically assigns team members to tasks based on status.
options.explanation: Assignment is manual; the property only shows status.

options.text: It consolidates all tasks into a single numeric progress bar.
options.explanation: No progress bar is generated by the status property alone.

options.text: It provides a clear, standardized view of how many projects are To Do, In Progress, or Complete, reducing the need to interpret varied labels.
options.explanation: Correct – standard categories let managers quickly gauge overall workload.

options.text: It hides tasks that are not in the Complete category.
options.explanation: The property does not hide tasks unless filtered to do so.
